svg.text: four timers, four failure modes -- retransmission (loss), persist (zero window), keepalive (silent peer), 2*MSL (TIME_WAIT)

g.text: Retransmission timer (RFC 6298)
SRTT_new = (1 - a) * R + a * SRTT_old
RTTVAR_new = (1 - b) * |SRTT - R| + b * RTTVAR_old
RTO = SRTT + max(G, K * RTTVAR)
floored at 1.0s, capped at 60.0s (a = 1/8, b = 1/4, K = 4)
Jacobson 1988 -- RTO tracks the recent mean AND variance of RTT samples.
Without the variance term, RTO = 2*RTT misfires when queues build up.
Karn's algorithm: skip RTT sampling for retransmitted segments;
double RTO each timeout up to 64 s, then reset on next fresh ack.
Fast retransmit + fast recovery (RFC 5681)
3 duplicate acks arrive -> single-packet loss inferred
retransmit immediately (do not wait for timer)
ssthresh = cwnd / 2
cwnd = ssthresh + 3 * MSS
stay in congestion avoidance (TCP Reno)
Tahoe: drops to slow start on any loss.
Reno: fast retransmit + fast recovery; saw-tooth pattern,
congestion window oscillates around the BD product.
Persist timer -- zero-window probes
defense against: receiver advertises WIN=0,
the window-update segment is lost
probe schedule: 5s, 10s, 20s, 40s, 60s, 60s, ...
each probe is a 1-byte segment with SEQ = SND.NXT - 1
receiver must respond with current WIN even if still 0
Modern stacks alternate between 1-byte and 0-byte probes.
Without the persist timer, the zero-window deadlock would last forever.
Keepalive + 2*MSL TIME_WAIT
keepalive_time = 7200 s (2 h idle before first probe)
keepalive_intvl = 75 s (between probes)
keepalive_probes = 9 (max probes)
total deadline ~ 2.16 hours of idle
TIME_WAIT = 2 * MSL = 60 s default
Keepalive is controversial -- adds overhead, can drop
NAT-bound connections that just paused. Prefer application
heartbeats when latency requirements are tight.
loss -> dup-acks 1,2,3 -> fast retransmit at t+RTO/4 (not t+RTO) -> fast recovery halves cwnd -> linear increase resumes
